content: Browser idle RPG combat animations typically use a layered approach: (1) Game state layer (pure data, no DOM), (2) Animation queue/sequence layer that consumes state diffs, (3) Render layer that applies CSS classes and manages DOM. The pattern used by games like IdleOn, Melvor Idle, and AdventureQuest 3D web variant is to separate combat state (server-authoritative) from presentation (client-side). Animation sequences are typically queued — a state diff arrives, gets translated into an ordered list of animation steps, each step is a function that registers CSS class additions/removals with timeouts or rAF frames. Key insight: animation systems should never directly mutate game state, only read it.
